1: The wheat harvest (qĕṣîr ḥiṭṭîm) places this episode in late spring, roughly May-June — about the time of the feast of Weeks (Shavuot, Pentecost). Samson's visit with a young goat (gĕdî ʿizzîm) as a gift follows ANE custom for reconciliation visits or visits to a spouse after an estrangement. The same gift-gesture appears in Judges 6:19 (Gideon to the angel) and Genesis 38:17 (Judah to Tamar). Bringing a goat to 'go in to his wife' uses the standard Hebrew euphemism for sexual relations.
2: Her father's explanation — 'I was sure you utterly hated her' (śānōʾ śĕnēʾtāh, the infinitive absolute for emphasis) — invokes the legal concept of divorce by abandonment. ANE marriage contracts typically specified that a husband who deserted his wife without support or divorce proceedings forfeited his rights. The father's offer of the younger sister as a replacement reflects the ANE practice of levirate-adjacent arrangements where family honor required providing the man with a wife. 'Isn't her younger sister more beautiful?' appeals to practical resolution.
3: Samson's declaration of blamelessness — niqqêtî (H5352, I am clean/innocent) — is a forensic term from covenant law. He is establishing that his subsequent action is legally justified retaliation, not unprovoked aggression. In the honor-shame culture of the ancient world, the sequence of offense (wife given away) → declaration of clean hands → proportional response was a recognized social script. The Philistines' action against him (via his wife) justifies his action against them.
4: The capture of 300 foxes (or jackals — the Hebrew šûʿālîm refers to both species) is the most discussed logistical element of the Samson narrative. Using animals to destroy crops was a recognized form of agricultural warfare: Song of Solomon 2:15 mentions foxes that destroy vineyards. The pairing of animals with firebrands and releasing them into grain fields exploits the animals' natural tendency to flee into cover (agricultural fields) when frightened. The scale (300 animals, paired in 150 pairs) is likely hyperbolic — the point is total destruction of the Philistine harvest, not a precise census of foxes.
5: The targets — standing grain, shocks of grain, and olive orchards — represent the complete agricultural wealth of a settlement. Standing grain was the unharvested crop; shocks were already cut and stacked for threshing; the orchards were the long-term capital investment (olive trees take 15-20 years to mature). Destroying all three meant the community would face both immediate hunger and multi-year agricultural recovery. This is total economic warfare, calibrated to inflict maximum long-term damage.
6: The Philistines' response — burning the Timnite woman and her father — is collective punishment, the standard ANE mechanism for penalizing a family for an offense committed by one of its members. They identify Samson's motive correctly (he was wronged by his wife's remarriage) and execute the offending family. This creates the next cycle: the Philistines kill the woman to punish Samson, who then attacks the Philistines again in retaliation. The narrative structure shows an escalating reciprocal honor-violence cycle characteristic of ANE tribal warfare.
7: Samson's vow — 'If this is what you do, I will certainly take revenge' — uses the oath formula 'im-lōʾ... ʿim' (surely/if-not). Revenge (nāqam, H5358) in the ancient world was not understood primarily as emotional retaliation but as the restoration of broken social order: when someone harmed you without justification, you had both the right and the social obligation to restore the honor balance. Samson's 'and after that I will stop' suggests he envisions a final reckoning, not ongoing warfare.
8: 'Hip and thigh' (šôq ʿal-yārēk) is a Hebrew idiom for thoroughness or severity in battle; the literal meaning (thigh upon hip) may describe a specific killing technique — pinning or disabling — but the phrase's idiomatic sense is 'a great and complete slaughter.' The retreat to the cave at Etam (identified in 2 Chronicles 11:6 as a fortified town in the Shephelah of Judah) shows Samson withdrawing to Judahite territory after his raid — seeking sanctuary within his own people's borders.
9: Lehi (H3896, jaw/jawbone — the name is aetiological, given after the battle described in Judges 15:15-17) was in the Shephelah, roughly in the territory of Judah near the border with the Philistines. The Philistine military expedition into Judah (wayyaʿălû pĕlištîm, they came up) shows that Samson's actions have triggered a full-scale military response: a single individual's guerrilla warfare has provoked an army-level incursion, demonstrating Samson's strategic significance to both sides.
10: The Judahites' question — 'why have you come up against us?' — and the Philistine answer — 'to bind Samson and do to him as he did to us' — frames this as a law-enforcement operation, not an invasion. The Philistines present themselves as seeking proportional justice, using legal language (kaʾăšer ʿāśāh lānû, as he did to us). The Judahites' response (going to bind Samson themselves, Judges 15:11-12) shows how deeply they have accepted Philistine overlordship: they are willing to deliver their own deliverer to avoid conflict with their occupiers.
11: 'Don't you know that the Philistines rule over us?' (lōʾ yādaʿtā kî mōšĕlîm bānû pĕlištîm) — the question reveals the depth of Judah's accommodation to foreign domination. By the period of Samson, Philistine domination had been normalized to the point where the tribe of Judah regarded a deliverer as a threat to social stability rather than a gift. This is precisely the spiritual condition that the Deuteronomic cycle diagnoses: when Israel stops crying out to YHWH (as they apparently do in the Samson narrative — no corporate cry of distress precedes his birth), they lose their freedom and eventually their desire for it.
12: The social prohibition against killing a member of your own people — 'We will only bind you and hand you over' — reflects the ANE tribal law of blood-covenant: shedding the blood of your own kinsman was a categorical violation that would create a blood-feud within Judah itself. The 3,000 men of Judah who come to bind Samson are acting within recognized social-legal constraints. Samson's one condition ('swear you won't kill me yourselves') accepts the binding in exchange for the guarantee against intra-tribal bloodshed.
13: The two new ropes (ḥăbālîm ḥădāšîm šĕnayim) are a symbol of the strongest ordinary restraint available. In ANE and later contexts, 'new' ropes or bonds that have never been used were considered to have maximum strength (not weakened by use). The same logic appears in Samson's later deceptions with Delilah: the tests use 'unused' or 'fresh' binding materials. The ropes of Judah, representing human effort to contain divine power, will prove no more effective than the ropes Delilah uses.
14: The Philistines' war cry (yĕrûʿû liqrāʾtô, shouted against him) as Samson arrives was an ANE intimidation technique — battle cries were used to frighten enemies before combat. The Spirit of YHWH rushing upon Samson at this moment (the same formula as Judges 13:25, Judges 14:6, Judges 14:19) transforms the Judahites' ropes into burnt flax — the contrast of fire imagery (Samson's weakness becoming as fire-consumed flax) with the fire of the foxes episode creates a recurring fire motif in the Samson cycle.
15: A fresh jawbone of a donkey (lĕḥî ḥămôr ṭərîyyāh) — the qualifier 'fresh' (ṭārî, H2961, moist/new) is practically significant: a dried jawbone would be brittle, while a fresh one retains its density and durability. Donkey jawbones are substantial — the lower mandible of a mature donkey weighs about 0.5 kg with a natural grip in the skull socket. As an improvised weapon, it functioned as a club/chopper rather than a blade. The weapon's choice reflects Samson's characteristic improvisation — he fights with what is at hand.
16: Samson's victory song (ḥămôr ḥămōr ātayim biḥlĕqat haḥămôr hikkêtî ʾelep ʾîš) is an untranslatable pun: ḥămôr means both 'donkey' and (possibly) 'heap,' while ḥămōr ātayim means 'two heaps/a donkey's heap.' The song celebrates the victory through wordplay, a common form of Hebrew victory poetry (cf. Deborah's song, Judges 5; Moses' song, Exod 15). Short victory songs (šîrê qĕṣer) were a standard form of ANE military commemoration, often tied to specific weapons or tactics.
17: Ramath-lehi (Height of the Jawbone) is an aetiological place-name created at the moment of victory. Place-name etiologies — where a place receives its name from a specific event — are a narrative device throughout Judges and Genesis: Beer-lahai-roi (Genesis 16:14), Mahanaim (Genesis 32:2), Penuel (Genesis 32:30), Beer (Judges 9:21). The jawbone's throwing away (wayyašlēk) marks the weapon as ritually significant — Samson discards it rather than keeping it as a trophy, perhaps recognizing it as a dead-animal object requiring disposal.
18: Samson's prayer — 'You have given this great deliverance by the hand of your servant' — is his first direct prayer in the narrative and uses formal covenant-address language: ʿaḇdekā (your servant) positions him as YHWH's vassal. The theological acknowledgment is followed immediately by existential crisis: extreme thirst after extraordinary physical exertion. The prayer's pattern (acknowledgment of divine action + personal need) mirrors many Psalms of thanksgiving (Psalms 118:5-6: 'In my distress I called on YHWH... YHWH is on my side; I will not fear').
19: The water miracle — God split open the hollow place (makteš, H4388, mortar/hollow) in Lehi — parallels Exodus 17:6 (water from the rock at Meribah) and Numbers 20:8-11 (Kadesh). The spring is named En-hakkore (H5875, Spring of the One Who Cries Out/the Caller) — aetiological again, the place named for Samson's prayer. The water miracle's location at the place of the jawbone victory links the two events: where Samson won by the Spirit's power, he is also sustained by God's provision. The name En-hakkore 'is in Lehi to this day' — an archival note authenticating the tradition.
20: Samson's twenty-year judgeship — the only tenure length given in the Samson narrative — is notably characterized as being 'in the days of the Philistines' rather than resulting in 'rest' (šāqaṭ, the standard Judges formula). Every other major judge gives the land rest (Judges 3:11, Judges 3:30, Judges 5:31, Judges 8:28); Samson does not. His deliverance is partial and personal, not communal and complete. The Philistine domination continues through his entire tenure and beyond — his death in Judges 16 does not produce liberation. This is the narrator's assessment of the Samson period: a deliverer who delivers himself more than his people.
